title_en: Structure and Organization of the Majmu
content_en:
Majmu al-Fatawa in its standard 37-volume form, edited by Abd al-Rahman ibn Muhammad ibn Qasim al-Asimi an-Najdi and his son Muhammad, is organized thematically rather than chronologically. The collection moves from theological subjects — faith, attributes of God, the Quran — through jurisprudential topics in roughly the order of classical fiqh manuals (purification, prayer, zakat, fasting, pilgrimage, commercial transactions, family law, criminal law) and then through a range of special topics including Sufism and spirituality, logic and philosophy, Quranic interpretation, and hadith sciences.

This thematic organization makes the collection significantly more useful as a reference than a chronological arrangement would have been. A scholar seeking Ibn Taymiyyah's position on a specific theological question or legal topic can navigate directly to the relevant section. The editors' organization reflects a sophisticated understanding of how scholars actually use fatawa collections — not by reading them sequentially but by consulting them for specific topics.

The content of the individual pieces varies widely. Some are short responses to specific questions (the fatawa in the narrow sense), answering a particular questioner's query in a few paragraphs. Others are full-length treatises (rasa'il) running to dozens or even hundreds of pages, addressing complex theological or legal questions with the comprehensive analysis that Ibn Taymiyyah brought to everything he wrote. The distinction between fatwa and treatise was fluid in his practice, as a question that interested him might prompt a substantial scholarly investigation well beyond what the immediate question required.

The coverage within each section reflects Ibn Taymiyyah's characteristic priorities. In theology, the divine attributes, the nature of God's will and knowledge, and the question of whether God's speech (the Quran) is eternal or created receive extensive treatment. In jurisprudence, topics where he disagreed with established school doctrine — divorce pronouncements, visiting graves, the permissibility of certain devotional practices — receive particular attention. In Sufi spirituality, he is both appreciative (of genuine asceticism and devotion) and sharply critical (of philosophical Sufism and ecstatic practices he considered unsupported by texts).
